principles.body: Kannada is written the way it sounds. Read the letters left to right and you have said the word.
principles.title: Say what you see

principles.body: A capital letter in our spellings (Ta, Da, La) means curl your tongue up and back — a fuller, hollow sound.
principles.title: Capitals curl your tongue

principles.body: A doubled consonant (appa, haLLi) is just held a beat longer than a single one.
principles.title: Doubled letters linger

consonantFamilies.hint: Sound starts at the back of the mouth.
Tongue presses the roof of the mouth.
Tongue curls up and back (the capital sounds).
Tongue tip touches the upper teeth (the lowercase sounds).

lessons.title: Greetings
lessons.situation: You have just met someone — a neighbour, a shopkeeper, someone at work. This is how you open.
lessons.realWorldPrompt: Next time you see your auto driver, building guard, or a shopkeeper — open with "namaskāra, heegiddira?" before anything else.
lessons.registerNote: Neutral = use with someone your age or younger. Respectful = use with elders or strangers.

lessons.title: Names
lessons.situation: You are meeting someone for the first time — at work, in your building, or at a social event.
lessons.realWorldPrompt: Next time you meet someone new, ask "nimma hesaru enu?" — even if they speak English back, you opened in Kannada.
lessons.registerNote: Neutral = use with someone your age or younger. Respectful = use with elders or strangers.

lessons.title: Wanting
lessons.situation: You are at a street market, vegetable vendor, or small shop. You want something, you want to know the price, and you want to negotiate.
lessons.realWorldPrompt: Next time you buy vegetables or anything from a street vendor, ask "idu estu?" instead of pointing at the price.
lessons.registerNote: Neutral = use with someone your age or younger. Respectful = use with elders or strangers.

lessons.title: Pointing
lessons.situation: You need to point something out, ask where something is, or describe a location.
lessons.realWorldPrompt: Next time you need to point something out, use "illi" and "alli" instead of just pointing silently.

lessons.title: Easy verbs
lessons.situation: You want to ask someone to do something, or describe what you or someone else is doing.
lessons.realWorldPrompt: Next time you want someone to do something, use the respectful form — "maadi", "hoogi", "noodi" — instead of English.
lessons.registerNote: Neutral = use with someone your age or younger. Respectful = use with elders or strangers.

lessons.title: Questions
lessons.situation: You need to ask a question — about a person, a place, a thing, or a situation.
lessons.realWorldPrompt: Next time you want to ask someone something, open with the Kannada question word — "yaaru", "enu", "elli" — even if the rest comes out in English.

lessons.title: Hard verbs
lessons.situation: More actions — reading, writing, working, sending. These come up every day.
lessons.realWorldPrompt: Next time someone asks what you are doing, answer in Kannada — "naanu kelasa maaduttiddene" or "naanu oduttiddene".
lessons.registerNote: Neutral = use with someone your age or younger. Respectful = use with elders or strangers.

lessons.title: Putting it together
lessons.situation: Putting everything together — greeting, names, shopping, questions. A real conversation from start to finish.
lessons.realWorldPrompt: Have a full Kannada conversation today — greeting, name, one question. Even 3 exchanges counts.
